title: Rectal bleeding documentation
source_review_basis: full authoritative documents inspected and archived; item-level retain/rewrite/remove/add comparisons recorded

assessment.text: Supports routine observations, full history and examination including digital rectal examination, appropriate blood tests, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, haemoglobin, and previous lower-GI-bleeding admission context.

source.title: Diagnosis and management of acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding: guidelines from the British Society of Gastroenterology
source.population: Adults aged 16 years and over presenting acutely to hospital with lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
source.setting: Acute hospital assessment and management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
source.exclusions: Exact only for selected acute adult hospital lower-GI-bleeding presentation, initial-assessment, antithrombotic-context, stability, and qualified follow-up fields. Uncomplicated primary-care bleeding, chronic bleeding, diagnosis, treatment, and UAE pathways remain outside scope or require clinician review.

exact_location.heading: Scope of the guideline and background presentation description

source.evidence_paraphrase: Defines acute adult hospital lower gastrointestinal bleeding as bright or dark red blood per rectum, clots per rectum, or blood mixed with stool, while warning that brisk bleeding should not automatically be ascribed to haemorrhoids.

assessment.text: Supports routine observations, full history and examination including digital rectal examination, appropriate blood tests, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, haemoglobin, and previous lower-GI-bleeding admission context.

assessment.text: Supports documenting whether anticoagulant or antiplatelet treatment is relevant to acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding; medicine interruption, reversal, and restart instructions are deliberately not mapped.

exact_location.heading: Abstract and recommendations — anticoagulant and antiplatelet context

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports documenting whether anticoagulant or antiplatelet treatment is relevant to acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding; medicine interruption, reversal, and restart instructions are deliberately not mapped.

assessment.text: Supports clinician assessment of haemodynamic stability, major versus minor bleeding context, and qualified clinician-decided hospital or outpatient follow-up documentation without calculating a score or inserting disposition advice.
assessment.rationale: The exact stability and follow-up section supports clinician-entered disposition, safety net, and follow-up documentation without assigning urgency.
